title: Frontend Cheat Sheet
subtitle: A compact revision guide for browser fundamentals, framework recall, and fast interview prep

sections.intro: Semantic structure first. It makes styling, accessibility, and SEO easier later.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html:
Semantic structure first. It makes styling, accessibility, and SEO easier later.

  • Use real landmarks, headings, buttons, and labels.
  • Native elements give you keyboard and browser behavior for free.
  • Markup meaning still matters under frameworks.

items.label: Focus
items.html: Prefer native semantic elements before generic div wrappers.

items.label: Why it matters
items.html: Because the browser, accessibility tree, SEO surface, and maintainability all depend on the actual markup you emit.

items.label: Watch out
items.html: Using headings or divs for visual styling only instead of real document meaning.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: Do not use ARIA or JavaScript to rebuild what native HTML already gives you well.

blocks.title: Fast recap
items: Landmarks and headings should describe structure.

qa.question: What should you remember about semantic HTML?
qa.answerHtml: Prefer native semantic elements before generic div wrappers.

qa.question: Why does semantic HTML matter in real frontend work?
qa.answerHtml: Because the browser, accessibility tree, SEO surface, and maintainability all depend on the actual markup you emit.

qa.question: What is the interview trap around semantic HTML?
qa.answerHtml: Using headings or divs for visual styling only instead of real document meaning.

sections.intro: Most CSS mistakes come from weak layout mental models, not from missing syntax.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html:
Most CSS mistakes come from weak layout mental models, not from missing syntax.

  • Use Flexbox for one-dimensional alignment.
  • Use Grid for coordinated row and column layout.
  • Gap is often cleaner than margin hacks.

items.label: Focus
items.html: Know when Flexbox, Grid, gap, sizing rules, and overflow behavior are the real answer.

items.label: Why it matters
items.html: Because real UI quality depends on layout reasoning more than on memorizing random properties.

items.label: Watch out
items.html: Fighting overflow and sizing bugs with fixed heights and random widths instead of understanding intrinsic sizing.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: If content can grow, understand sizing and overflow before adding more CSS band-aids.

blocks.title: Fast recap
items: Gap is cleaner than sibling-margin hacks.
Sizing rules explain many layout surprises.

qa.question: What should you remember about CSS layout?
qa.answerHtml: Know when Flexbox, Grid, gap, sizing rules, and overflow behavior are the real answer.

qa.question: Why does CSS layout matter in real frontend work?
qa.answerHtml: Because real UI quality depends on layout reasoning more than on memorizing random properties.

qa.question: What is the interview trap around CSS layout?
qa.answerHtml: Fighting overflow and sizing bugs with fixed heights and random widths instead of understanding intrinsic sizing.

sections.intro: JavaScript interview misses usually come from mental-model gaps, not syntax gaps.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html:
JavaScript interview misses usually come from mental-model gaps, not syntax gaps.

  • Keep closures, value vs reference, and async ordering hot.
  • Understand modules and objects, not only syntax.
  • Many output puzzles are event-loop or scope questions in disguise.

items.label: Focus
items.html: Retain the language ideas that explain most browser and framework behavior: scope, closures, async flow, identity, and modules.

items.label: Why it matters
items.html: Because those concepts explain runtime bugs and interview puzzles far better than memorized syntax alone.

items.label: Watch out
items.html: Memorizing outputs without understanding the event loop, scope, or object identity underneath them.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: If you cannot explain why the code runs that way, you do not really know the answer yet.

blocks.title: Fast recap
items: Strict equality is the safer default.

qa.question: What should you remember about JavaScript fundamentals?
qa.answerHtml: Retain the language ideas that explain most browser and framework behavior: scope, closures, async flow, identity, and modules.

qa.question: Why does JavaScript fundamentals matter in real frontend work?
qa.answerHtml: Because those concepts explain runtime bugs and interview puzzles far better than memorized syntax alone.

qa.question: What is the interview trap around JavaScript fundamentals?
qa.answerHtml: Memorizing outputs without understanding the event loop, scope, or object identity underneath them.

sections.intro: Frontend work is not only frameworks. It still depends on browser events, networking, storage, and DOM behavior.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html:
Frontend work is not only frameworks. It still depends on browser events, networking, storage, and DOM behavior.

  • Event propagation still causes real UI bugs.
  • Fetch, storage, and DOM APIs are still the platform.
  • Framework abstractions leak during debugging.

items.label: Focus
items.html: Remember the basics of DOM events, fetch, storage, event delegation, and browser-side state boundaries.

items.label: Why it matters
items.html: Because many real bugs only make sense once you reason at the browser API layer instead of at the framework layer.

items.label: Watch out
items.html: Confusing bubbling or browser state behavior with application-state bugs.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: Do not assume the framework swallowed the platform. The browser is still underneath every component tree.

blocks.title: Fast recap
items: The DOM is still the platform underneath your framework.

qa.question: What should you remember about browser APIs?
qa.answerHtml: Remember the basics of DOM events, fetch, storage, event delegation, and browser-side state boundaries.

qa.question: Why does browser APIs matter in real frontend work?
qa.answerHtml: Because many real bugs only make sense once you reason at the browser API layer instead of at the framework layer.

qa.question: What is the interview trap around browser APIs?
qa.answerHtml: Confusing bubbling or browser state behavior with application-state bugs.

sections.intro: Accessibility quick wins prevent a surprising number of product and interview mistakes.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html:
Accessibility quick wins prevent a surprising number of product and interview mistakes.

  • Use semantic elements and visible focus.
  • Every field needs a real label.
  • Keyboard access is a product requirement, not a bonus.

items.label: Focus
items.html: Remember the high-frequency rules around semantic HTML, focus visibility, labeling, and keyboard access.

items.label: Why it matters
items.html: Because accessibility problems often begin with a few repeated mistakes that are cheap to prevent early.

items.label: Watch out
items.html: Using ARIA to patch wrong markup instead of starting with the correct native element and behavior.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: Do not remove focus indicators or replace labels with placeholders and call it done.

qa.question: What should you remember about accessibility quick rules?
qa.answerHtml: Remember the high-frequency rules around semantic HTML, focus visibility, labeling, and keyboard access.

qa.question: Why does accessibility quick rules matter in real frontend work?
qa.answerHtml: Because accessibility problems often begin with a few repeated mistakes that are cheap to prevent early.

qa.question: What is the interview trap around accessibility quick rules?
qa.answerHtml: Using ARIA to patch wrong markup instead of starting with the correct native element and behavior.

sections.intro: Frontend performance wins usually come from a handful of repeated habits.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html:
Frontend performance wins usually come from a handful of repeated habits.

  • Ship less JavaScript.
  • Optimize images and rendering strategy early.
  • Measure before optimizing details.

items.label: Focus
items.html: Think first about bundle size, rendering strategy, images, and runtime work instead of random micro-optimizations.

items.label: Why it matters
items.html: Because the biggest performance wins usually come from removing major browser work rather than tweaking one hook or style rule.

items.label: Watch out
items.html: Optimizing what you have not measured or obsessing over tiny code changes while large assets and rendering choices dominate cost.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: Do not call something fast because it feels fast on your own laptop. Measure user-facing performance instead.

blocks.title: Fast recap
items: Rendering strategy changes performance more than micro-tweaks often do.

qa.question: What should you remember about frontend performance?
qa.answerHtml: Think first about bundle size, rendering strategy, images, and runtime work instead of random micro-optimizations.

qa.question: Why does frontend performance matter in real frontend work?
qa.answerHtml: Because the biggest performance wins usually come from removing major browser work rather than tweaking one hook or style rule.

qa.question: What is the interview trap around frontend performance?
qa.answerHtml: Optimizing what you have not measured or obsessing over tiny code changes while large assets and rendering choices dominate cost.

sections.intro: React quick recall should focus on rendering, state, effects, refs, and boundaries.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html:
React quick recall should focus on rendering, state, effects, refs, and boundaries.

  • Render should stay pure.
  • State is a snapshot over time.
  • Effects are for synchronization, not ordinary calculations.

items.label: Focus
items.html: Keep the high-frequency React ideas hot: render, state, refs, effects, Context, and component boundaries.

items.label: Why it matters
items.html: Because many React bugs and interview misses happen when developers blur render logic, state changes, and external synchronization.

items.label: Watch out
items.html: Using Effects for values that could have been derived during render or treating refs like normal reactive state.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: If you can derive it during render, you usually do not need an Effect for it.

qa.question: What should you remember about React quick recall?
qa.answerHtml: Keep the high-frequency React ideas hot: render, state, refs, effects, Context, and component boundaries.

qa.question: Why does React quick recall matter in real frontend work?
qa.answerHtml: Because many React bugs and interview misses happen when developers blur render logic, state changes, and external synchronization.

qa.question: What is the interview trap around React quick recall?
qa.answerHtml: Using Effects for values that could have been derived during render or treating refs like normal reactive state.

sections.intro: Next.js revision should focus on server-first thinking, App Router structure, rendering, and caching.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html:
Next.js revision should focus on server-first thinking, App Router structure, rendering, and caching.

  • Server Components are the default mental model.
  • Client boundaries should be intentional.
  • Caching and rendering are connected decisions.

items.label: Focus
items.html: Remember App Router file conventions, server/client boundaries, rendering choices, and cache tradeoffs.

items.label: Why it matters
items.html: Because modern Next.js questions are really about where code runs and how freshness, latency, and bundle cost trade off.

items.label: Watch out
items.html: Moving components client-side by habit or talking about caching without knowing what actually needs to stay fresh.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: Every unnecessary client boundary increases bundle and hydration cost.

blocks.title: Fast recap
items: App Router uses file conventions heavily.

qa.question: What should you remember about Next.js quick recall?
qa.answerHtml: Remember App Router file conventions, server/client boundaries, rendering choices, and cache tradeoffs.

qa.question: Why does Next.js quick recall matter in real frontend work?
qa.answerHtml: Because modern Next.js questions are really about where code runs and how freshness, latency, and bundle cost trade off.

qa.question: What is the interview trap around Next.js quick recall?
qa.answerHtml: Moving components client-side by habit or talking about caching without knowing what actually needs to stay fresh.

sections.intro: Frontend workflow basics still depend on version control, reproducible installs, and small reviewable changes.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html:
Frontend workflow basics still depend on version control, reproducible installs, and small reviewable changes.

  • Use focused commits and fetch before integrating when possible.
  • Respect the lockfile.
  • Run project scripts instead of hidden global tools.

items.label: Focus
items.html: Keep Git, package-management, and review hygiene strong enough that frontend work stays reproducible and reviewable.

items.label: Why it matters
items.html: Because hidden machine state and vague Git habits make UI teams slower and create avoidable build failures.

items.label: Watch out
items.html: Normalizing force pushes, hidden global tools, or uncommitted lockfile changes in team workflows.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: If the build depends on your personal machine state, it is not a healthy workflow yet.

qa.question: What should you remember about frontend workflow basics?
qa.answerHtml: Keep Git, package-management, and review hygiene strong enough that frontend work stays reproducible and reviewable.

qa.question: Why does frontend workflow basics matter in real frontend work?
qa.answerHtml: Because hidden machine state and vague Git habits make UI teams slower and create avoidable build failures.

qa.question: What is the interview trap around frontend workflow basics?
qa.answerHtml: Normalizing force pushes, hidden global tools, or uncommitted lockfile changes in team workflows.

sections.intro: This final sheet is for fast interview pressure: quick definitions, debugging prompts, and the traps people answer too loosely.

blocks.title: Quick scan
blocks.html: Focus on short accurate answers first, then add the deeper tradeoff only if the interviewer asks. Clarity beats rambling.

blocks.title: Fast interview structure
blocks.code:
1. Define the concept clearly.
2. Explain why it exists.
3. Name the tradeoff or common bug.
4. Give one concrete example.
blocks.caption: A four-step structure keeps answers sharp under pressure.

items.label: Weak answer
items.html: Buzzwords with no mechanism or tradeoff.

items.label: Better answer
items.html: Definition plus one real example.

items.label: Strong answer
items.html: Definition, mechanism, tradeoff, and debugging awareness.

blocks.title: Common trap
blocks.html: Do not answer every frontend question at the framework layer. Many interviewers are checking whether you still understand the browser and platform underneath.

qa.question: Why is semantic HTML still an interview topic?
qa.answerHtml: Because markup meaning still affects accessibility, SEO, browser behavior, and maintainability regardless of framework use.

qa.question: Why is shipping less JavaScript often faster?
qa.answerHtml: The browser has less code to download, parse, compile, and execute.

qa.question: What is the practical difference between state and refs in React?
qa.answerHtml: State drives rendering while refs hold mutable values without triggering a render.

qa.question: Why is fetch knowledge still important in a framework-heavy stack?
qa.answerHtml: The browser networking model and async behavior still affect UI correctness and debugging.

qa.question: Why is 'just use ARIA' often a weak accessibility answer?
qa.answerHtml: Good accessibility usually starts with the correct native element and behavior.

qa.question: Why can Next.js caching questions be tricky?
qa.answerHtml: The right answer depends on what must stay fresh, what can be reused, and where the work runs.

qa.question: Why do interviewers ask about the event loop in frontend interviews?
qa.answerHtml: Async ordering affects rendering, state updates, fetch handling, and debugging even in UI-heavy roles.

qa.question: What is the first thing to say when asked to improve a slow page?
qa.answerHtml: Measure first, then target the real bottleneck rather than guessing.

qa.question: Why is a global CLI dependency a workflow smell?
qa.answerHtml: It makes local behavior depend on hidden machine state that CI and teammates may not share.

qa.question: Why does layout knowledge matter beyond CSS trivia?
qa.answerHtml: Real UI quality depends on sizing, overflow, alignment, and responsiveness, not just memorizing properties.

qa.question: What makes a frontend answer sound senior?
qa.answerHtml: It connects browser behavior, user impact, performance tradeoffs, and debugging strategy instead of naming only framework APIs.

qa.question: Why is 'semantic HTML is good for SEO' an incomplete answer?
qa.answerHtml: The deeper point is that semantics affect structure, accessibility, browser behavior, and maintainability too.

qa.question: How do you avoid rambling in frontend interviews?
qa.answerHtml: Define the concept, explain the mechanism, name the tradeoff, and give one example.

qa.question: What is the strongest quick correction to 'frontend is mostly design now'?
qa.answerHtml: Modern frontend still requires runtime reasoning, platform understanding, performance strategy, accessibility, and system integration.

qa.question: Why is the cheat-sheet mindset useful even for senior engineers?
qa.answerHtml: Fast recall under pressure still benefits from compressed mental models and recurring decision rules.

qa.question: How would you define frontend mastery in one sentence?
qa.answerHtml: Frontend mastery means understanding the browser platform, UI architecture, runtime behavior, and user experience deeply enough to make strong tradeoffs under pressure.

qa.question: What does a great revision sheet optimize for?
qa.answerHtml: Fast recall of high-frequency concepts, tradeoffs, and traps without losing technical accuracy.

qa.question: Why should senior frontend engineers still revisit fundamentals?
qa.answerHtml: Because browser semantics, async behavior, layout reasoning, and accessibility still shape real product quality under every framework.
